name: Eira Lizeth Corral Sepúlveda
bio: Eira Lizeth Corral Sepúlveda is the first Latina elected to the MWRD Board of Commissioners, inaugurated December 8, 2020 — also the youngest Commissioner and first in recent history to come from the Northwest suburbs of Cook County. A 'hometown proud' elected official from Hanover Park, she served three terms as Hanover Park Village Clerk beginning at age 23 as a single mother, from 2009 to 2021. She has 12+ years of public service experience, has registered thousands of voters, and champions environmental equity, inclusion, and infrastructure investment. She holds a B.A. (double major: Latino & Latin American Studies and Political Science) from DePaul University (2007) and a graduate degree from University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign (2011). She is running for re-election on March 17, 2026 with endorsements from both U.S. senators, eight U.S. representatives, the Chicago Tribune editorial board, the Chicago Sun-Times editorial board, and the Cook County Democratic Party.
prior_office: MWRD Commissioner (elected 2020–present, term ends Dec 8 2026). Hanover Park Village Clerk, three terms (2009–2021). Member: CMAP Human Relations Committee; Metropolitan Mayors Conference Diversity Taskforce; Northwest Municipal Conference; Illinois Census Commission.

key_votes.summary: Championed expansion of green infrastructure programs including downspout disconnection education and permeable surface installations.

key_votes.bill: MWRD Deep Tunnel / McCook Reservoir Completion
key_votes.summary: Supports completing the Deep Tunnel project to expand flood retention capacity to 19 billion gallons of stormwater.
Advanced nutrient reduction strategy (phosphorus, nitrogen) at water reclamation plants to protect Chicago-area waterways.
Affirmed MWRD's commitment to Affirmative Action policies and expanded Small Business Enterprise and Mentor Protégé programs.

policy_platform.position: Strengthen infrastructure investments for climate resilience — complete Deep Tunnel to 19 billion gallon capacity; protect waterways from polluters.
Equitable inclusion of diverse communities in infrastructure investments and economic opportunities. Continue funding MWRD Environmental Justice Division and Community Partnership Councils.
Enterprise resilience — alleviate taxpayer burden by advancing 'polluters pay' model; evaluate water reuse revenue opportunities.
Expand green infrastructure programs; downspout disconnection education for property owners.
Nutrient reduction strategy (phosphorus, nitrogen) at water reclamation plants.
Community ambassador role — annual Monarch Fests and grassroots outreach; expand community engagement to build trust.

career_history.org: Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago
career_history.highlight: First Latina elected to MWRD board; inaugurated December 8, 2020. Youngest Commissioner and first from Northwest Cook County suburbs in recent history.
Elected at age 23 as single mother. Served three terms. Promoted environmentalism in village government; strengthened Hanover Park's Tree City USA designation; established Arbor Day initiatives.

career_history.org: CMAP Human Relations Committee; Metropolitan Mayors Conference Diversity Taskforce; Northwest Municipal Conference; Illinois Census Commission
career_history.highlight: Represents regional diversity and equity interests across multiple governmental bodies.

endorsements: Chicago NOW (Chicago National Organization of Women)

social_pulse.summary: Steady, positive profile as an incumbent with deep suburban and city-wide support. Heavy institutional endorsement slate. Coverage from Tribune, Sun-Times, WTTW. First Latina MWRD Commissioner story well-covered.
